items.q: Which letter is typically SILENT at the end of French words like 'petit' or 'froid'?
items.why: Students try to pronounce every letter. Final consonants in French are usually silent (CaReFuL exceptions: c, r, f, l). Petit is pronounced 'puh-TEE', not 'puh-TEET'.

items.q: How do you say 'I am using my phone right now' (emphasising the action is in progress)?
items.correct: Je suis en train d'utiliser mon portable.
items.why: Students translate English '-ing' as a participle. French has no progressive tense - the present 'j'utilise' covers both 'I use' and 'I am using'. For emphasis on the ongoing action, use 'être en train de + infinitive'.

items.q: How do you say 'I'm going to my friend's house' in French?
items.misconception: Je vais à la maison de mon ami.
items.why: Students translate word-for-word using 'à la maison de'. 'Chez' specifically means 'at the home/place of' someone: chez moi, chez le médecin, chez Pierre. Much more idiomatic and tested.
It's mercredi. “mardi” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: How would you say 'a green car' in French?
items.why: Correct answer: une voiture verte. “un vert voiture” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.
The real figure is 17. The wrong options cluster deliberately close — anchor the exact number to one concrete fact so it can't drift.
